metadata.description: Real paper excerpts for VVUQ benchmarking - quantum optics, solid state, RF engineering

test_cases.title: Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian describing a two-level atom coupled to a single cavity mode is:
$$\hat{H} = \hbar\omega_c \hat{a}^\dagger\hat{a} + \frac{\hbar\omega_a}{2}\hat{\sigma}_z + \hbar g(\hat{a}^\dagger\hat{\sigma}_- + \hat{a}\hat{\sigma}_+)$$
where $g$ is the vacuum Rabi coupling strength, $\omega_c$ is the cavity frequency, and $\omega_a$ is the atomic transition frequency.

test_cases.title: Jaynes-Cummings - WRONG COUPLING TERM
test_cases.content:
The Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian is:
$$\hat{H} = \hbar\omega_c \hat{a}^\dagger\hat{a} + \frac{\hbar\omega_a}{2}\hat{\sigma}_z + \hbar g(\hat{a}^\dagger\hat{\sigma}_+ + \hat{a}\hat{\sigma}_-)$$
where the coupling term describes the atom-cavity interaction.

errors.description: Coupling term has wrong operator pairing. Should be a†σ- + aσ+ (rotating wave approximation). The given form a†σ+ + aσ- violates energy conservation and the RWA.

test_cases.title: Purcell Factor - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The Purcell factor describing the enhancement of spontaneous emission in a cavity is:
$$F_P = \frac{3Q\lambda^3}{4\pi^2 V}$$
where $Q$ is the cavity quality factor, $\lambda$ is the wavelength, and $V$ is the mode volume.

test_cases.title: Purcell Factor - MISSING FACTOR
test_cases.content:
The Purcell enhancement factor is given by:
$$F_P = \frac{Q\lambda^3}{4\pi^2 V}$$
This shows that high-Q, small mode volume cavities maximize spontaneous emission enhancement.

errors.description: Missing factor of 3 in numerator. Correct form is F_P = 3Qλ³/(4π²V). The factor of 3 comes from averaging over dipole orientations.

test_cases.title: NV Center Zero-Field Splitting - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The ground state spin Hamiltonian for the NV⁻ center in diamond is:
$$\hat{H} = D(S_z^2 - \frac{S(S+1)}{3}) + \gamma_e \vec{B} \cdot \vec{S}$$
where $D \approx 2.87$ GHz is the zero-field splitting and $\gamma_e = 28$ GHz/T is the electron gyromagnetic ratio.

test_cases.title: NV Magnetometry Sensitivity - WRONG SCALING
test_cases.content:
The magnetic field sensitivity of an NV magnetometer scales as:
$$\eta_B \propto \frac{1}{\gamma_e \sqrt{C \cdot T_2^* \cdot t}}$$
where $C$ is the photon collection efficiency, $T_2^*$ is the dephasing time, and $t$ is the total measurement time. For $N$ NV centers measured in parallel, the sensitivity improves as $1/N$.

errors.description: For N independent NV centers, sensitivity improves as 1/√N (shot noise limit), not 1/N. The 1/N scaling would require entanglement (Heisenberg limit).

test_cases.title: Cooperativity Parameter - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The single-atom cooperativity in cavity QED is defined as:
$$C = \frac{g^2}{\kappa \gamma}$$
where $g$ is the atom-cavity coupling, $\kappa$ is the cavity decay rate, and $\gamma$ is the atomic spontaneous emission rate. Strong coupling requires $C > 1$.

test_cases.title: Fermi-Dirac Distribution - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The probability of occupation of a state at energy $E$ is given by the Fermi-Dirac distribution:
$$f(E) = \frac{1}{e^{(E-E_F)/k_BT} + 1}$$
where $E_F$ is the Fermi energy, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ is temperature.

test_cases.title: Fermi-Dirac - SIGN ERROR
test_cases.content:
The Fermi-Dirac distribution function is:
$$f(E) = \frac{1}{e^{(E_F-E)/k_BT} + 1}$$
At $T=0$, this gives a step function at the Fermi level.

errors.description: Exponent sign is inverted. Should be (E-E_F), not (E_F-E). The given form would give f=1 for E>>E_F and f=0 for E<<E_F, which is backwards.

test_cases.title: Effective Mass Approximation - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
Near a band extremum, the electron energy can be approximated as:
$$E(k) = E_c + \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m^*}$$
where the effective mass is defined by:
$$\frac{1}{m^*} = \frac{1}{\hbar^2}\frac{d^2E}{dk^2}$$

test_cases.title: Debye Model Specific Heat - LOW-T LIMIT ERROR
test_cases.content:
The Debye model predicts that at low temperatures ($T << \Theta_D$), the specific heat of a solid varies as:
$$C_V = \frac{12\pi^4}{5}Nk_B\left(\frac{T}{\Theta_D}\right)^2$$
This is the famous Debye $T^2$ law.

errors.description: Debye low-T specific heat scales as T³, not T². The correct form is C_V ∝ (T/Θ_D)³. The T² dependence would apply to 2D systems.

test_cases.title: Diamond Bandgap - CORRECT
test_cases.content: Diamond has an indirect bandgap of approximately 5.47 eV at room temperature. This large bandgap makes diamond transparent to visible light and gives it excellent electrical insulating properties.

test_cases.title: S-Parameter Definition - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
For a two-port network, the S-parameters are defined as:
$$S_{11} = \frac{b_1}{a_1}\bigg|_{a_2=0}, \quad S_{21} = \frac{b_2}{a_1}\bigg|_{a_2=0}$$
where $a_i$ are incident wave amplitudes and $b_i$ are reflected wave amplitudes, with port 2 terminated in $Z_0$.

test_cases.title: Noise Figure - WRONG FORMULA
test_cases.content:
The noise figure of a cascaded system is given by:
$$F_{total} = F_1 + F_2 + F_3 + ...$$
where $F_i$ is the noise figure of the $i$-th stage.

errors.description: Missing gain terms. Friis formula is F_total = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + (F_3-1)/(G_1·G_2) + ... where G_i is the gain of stage i. Later stages contribute less if early stages have high gain.

test_cases.title: Transmission Line Impedance - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The characteristic impedance of a lossless transmission line is:
$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$
where $L$ is the inductance per unit length and $C$ is the capacitance per unit length.

test_cases.title: Q-Factor of Resonator - MISSING 2π
test_cases.content:
The quality factor of an LC resonator is defined as:
$$Q = \frac{f_0}{\Delta f} = \omega_0 RC$$
where $f_0$ is the resonant frequency and $\Delta f$ is the 3dB bandwidth.

errors.description: For a parallel RLC circuit, Q = R/(ω₀L) = ω₀RC. The formula Q = ω₀RC is correct for parallel RLC, but the first equality f₀/Δf applies to series RLC. Mixing definitions from different circuit topologies.

test_cases.title: Taylor Series Truncation - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The forward difference approximation for the first derivative is:
$$f'(x) \approx \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} + O(h)$$
with first-order truncation error proportional to $h$.

test_cases.title: Central Difference - WRONG ORDER
test_cases.content:
The central difference approximation for the first derivative is:
$$f'(x) \approx \frac{f(x+h) - f(x-h)}{2h} + O(h)$$
This is first-order accurate like the forward difference.

errors.description: Central difference is O(h²) accurate, not O(h). The symmetric stencil cancels the O(h) error term. This is why central differences are preferred despite requiring more function evaluations.

test_cases.title: CFL Condition - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
For the explicit FDTD method, the Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy (CFL) stability condition requires:
$$c\Delta t \leq \frac{\Delta x}{\sqrt{d}}$$
where $c$ is the wave speed, $\Delta t$ is the time step, $\Delta x$ is the spatial step, and $d$ is the number of spatial dimensions.

test_cases.title: FDTD Dispersion - WRONG CLAIM
test_cases.content: The Yee FDTD algorithm is dispersion-free for all wavelengths as long as the CFL condition is satisfied. This makes it ideal for broadband simulations without any numerical artifacts.

errors.description: FDTD has numerical dispersion that depends on propagation direction and wavelength. The phase velocity error scales as (Δx/λ)². For accurate results, typically need λ/Δx > 10-20. CFL ensures stability, not dispersion-free propagation.

test_cases.title: Commutator Relation - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The canonical commutation relation for position and momentum operators is:
$$[\hat{x}, \hat{p}] = i\hbar$$
This fundamental relation leads to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle.

test_cases.title: Spin Commutator - WRONG FACTOR
test_cases.content:
The commutation relations for spin operators are:
$$[S_x, S_y] = i S_z$$
These cyclic relations define the angular momentum algebra.

errors.description: Missing ℏ factor. Correct form is [Sₓ, Sᵧ] = iℏSᵤ. The dimensionless version [σₓ, σᵧ] = 2iσᵤ uses Pauli matrices with factor of 2, not 1.

test_cases.title: Density Matrix Evolution - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The evolution of the density matrix under the Lindblad master equation is:
$$\frac{d\rho}{dt} = -\frac{i}{\hbar}[H, \rho] + \sum_k \gamma_k \left(L_k \rho L_k^\dagger - \frac{1}{2}\{L_k^\dagger L_k, \rho\}\right)$$
where $L_k$ are the Lindblad jump operators and $\gamma_k$ are decay rates.

test_cases.title: Lindblad - WRONG ANTICOMMUTATOR
test_cases.content:
The Lindblad master equation for open system dynamics is:
$$\frac{d\rho}{dt} = -\frac{i}{\hbar}[H, \rho] + \sum_k \gamma_k \left(L_k \rho L_k^\dagger + \frac{1}{2}\{L_k^\dagger L_k, \rho\}\right)$$

errors.description: Wrong sign on anticommutator term. Should be -½{L†L, ρ}, not +½{L†L, ρ}. The negative sign ensures trace preservation: Tr(dρ/dt) = 0.

test_cases.title: Carnot Efficiency - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
The maximum efficiency of a heat engine operating between hot reservoir at $T_H$ and cold reservoir at $T_C$ is:
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$
where temperatures are in Kelvin.

test_cases.title: Carnot Efficiency - CELSIUS ERROR
test_cases.content:
A heat engine operates between a hot source at 500°C and cold sink at 25°C. The Carnot efficiency is:
$$\eta = 1 - \frac{25}{500} = 95\%$$
This represents the theoretical maximum efficiency.

errors.description: Must use Kelvin, not Celsius. Correct calculation: η = 1 - (25+273)/(500+273) = 1 - 298/773 = 61.4%. Using Celsius gives meaningless result.

test_cases.title: Maxwell Faraday - CORRECT
test_cases.content:
Faraday's law in differential form is:
$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$
A time-varying magnetic field induces a rotational electric field.

test_cases.title: Poynting Vector - WRONG FORMULA
test_cases.content:
The Poynting vector representing electromagnetic energy flux is:
$$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{B}/\mu_0$$
with units of W/m².

errors.description: Poynting vector uses cross product, not dot product: S = E × B/μ₀ (or E × H). The dot product would give a scalar, not a vector, and has wrong physical meaning.
